analysis: The top-performing heuristics balance global greedy selection with local swap refinements. They prune dominated candidates early and reuse Monte Carlo samples via vectorized, chunk‐based computations to efficiently estimate marginal hypervolume contributions. Lazy updates (using heaps or incremental mask updates) minimize redundant calculations, reducing overhead. In contrast, weaker versions tend to recompute hypervolumes repeatedly without effective data reuse. The best designs separate candidate pruning, approximate evaluation, and focused exact refinements to achieve a strong balance between solution quality and runtime.
experience: Focus on aggressive pruning and vectorized, lazy-updating of contribution estimates. Reuse sampled data for both approximation and local search. Integrate global greedy selection with a focused exact-swap phase on a shortlist to cut overhead while still polishing hypervolume performance.
current_reflection: Focus on aggressive pruning and vectorized, lazy-updating of contribution estimates. Reuse sampled data for both approximation and local search. Integrate global greedy selection with a focused exact-swap phase on a shortlist to cut overhead while still polishing hypervolume performance.
bad_reflections: Focus on aggressive pre-pruning and reuse shared samples with vectorized, chunked computations. Incorporate a two-stage approach: fast greedy selection with approximate estimates followed by a brief, exact local refinement on a shortlist to improve quality without incurring excessive overhead.

Focus on candidate pruning, reuse of sampled data, and vectorized incremental updates. Minimize repeated expensive hypervolume calls by using approximate evaluations and limited local refinements on shortlisted candidates.

Focus on data reuse and vectorized updates. Use shared samples and lazy evaluations for marginal gains while combining aggressive candidate pruning with local refinements to improve both accuracy and runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
• Keywords: Aggressive pruning; vectorized lazy updates; data reuse; global greedy selection; targeted exact-swap refinement; hypervolume optimization.  
• Advice: Use vectorized, lazy-updating estimates and reuse sampled data for both approximation and local search. Employ a global greedy phase followed by an exact-swap step on a shortlist.  
• Avoid: Excessive evaluation overhead and repeatedly performing full hypervolume calculations.  
• Explanation: Combining approximate, vectorized updates with focused local refinements maximizes efficiency and accuracy by pruning candidates early and reducing expensive computations.
